stories.headline: Libyans blockade UNHCR's Tripoli office in anti-migrant protests as the UN denies any resettlement plans
stories.summary: On June 4, hundreds of Libyans blocked the UN refugee agency's Tripoli headquarters, demanding undocumented migrants leave and the office close, chanting 'Libya belongs to Libyans.' UNHCR responded June 5 denying it runs any migrant-resettlement program in Libya. Libya hosts roughly 900,000 migrants among about 7 million people.

perspectives.keyFraming: State media centers the UN's denial and rumor management, framing the episode as institutional reassurance rather than xenophobic mobilization.
perspectives.summary: Chinese state media reported both the protest shutting the UNHCR office and the UN mission's subsequent denial of any migrant-resettlement program, foregrounding the agency's clarification.

sources.title: UN mission denies allegations of migrant resettlement in Libya

stories.headline: Bolivia nears state of emergency as five-week blockade siege of La Paz triggers resignations and oil-field seizures
stories.summary: A protest movement that began in early May over fuel shortages, high inflation and austerity has hardened into scores of road blockades across seven departments, with at least nine reported dead and roughly $1.6 billion in losses. On June 3 President Rodrigo Paz sent a state-of-emergency bill to the Assembly as two ministers resigned; days later protesters briefly seized an oil field as La Paz hospitals declared an oxygen emergency.

perspectives.keyFraming: Centers the humanitarian toll — blocked ambulances, oxygen-starved hospitals — and indigenous and labor demands rather than the 'attempted coup' framing.
perspectives.summary: Latin American coverage foregrounded the human cost of the blockades, including deaths from blocked ambulances and a medical-oxygen emergency, while detailing the sectoral and indigenous grievances driving five weeks of unrest against the Paz government.

sources.title: Bolivia's blockade crisis reopens a deep racial divide

stories.headline: Mali's junta places roughly €3m bounty on al-Qaeda Sahel leader Iyad Ag Ghali after April mega-offensive
stories.summary: On June 5 Mali's military government offered a 2 billion CFA franc (about €3m/$3.5m) reward for the capture or 'neutralisation' of JNIM chief Iyad Ag Ghali, plus 1.5 billion CFA for his deputy Amadou Koufa. It follows JNIM's April 25 coordinated assault on Bamako, Kati and four other cities that killed Mali's defence minister, the largest jihadist operation since 2012.

perspectives.keyFraming: African outlets read the bounty as an admission the junta cannot win militarily and is now openly pricing the heads of leaders it once negotiated with — an angle US wires skip.
perspectives.summary: African coverage emphasized the bounty signals the junta's difficulty after the April 25 offensive reached Bamako and killed the defence minister. Ag Ghali is a former Malian diplomat already under a US terror designation; the reward scheme also targets Tuareg FLA commanders, underscoring the alliance reshaping the war. US front pages carried no coverage of the announcement.

sources.title: Mali junta offers $3.5M reward for jihadist leader

stories.headline: South Africa braces for June 30 anti-migrant 'national shutdown' as Ramaphosa rejects xenophobia label
stories.summary: Anti-immigration movements including Operation Dudula have set a June 30 ultimatum for undocumented migrants to leave and threatened a national shutdown that officials fear could echo the 2021 riots. On June 5 the Presidency ruled out any shutdown and warned against vigilantism, and Ramaphosa, speaking during Kenyan President Ruto's state visit, insisted 'South Africans are not xenophobic' while pledging tougher enforcement. Other African governments have begun repatriating nationals.

perspectives.keyFraming: US wires miss that this is a state-vs-vigilante sovereignty crisis: the government is racing to pre-empt a citizen 'shutdown' that could trigger lethal violence and a regional diplomatic rupture across Africa.
perspectives.summary: South African and pan-African outlets frame the June 30 ultimatum as a test of whether Pretoria can contain organized xenophobic vigilantism without conceding to demands for a moratorium on refugee applications. Coverage emphasizes Ramaphosa's defensive posture during Ruto's visit, the economic threat of a shutdown, and other African governments beginning to repatriate citizens.

sources.title: Ramaphosa to address South Africa's migration crisis as Ntshavheni rules out shutdown

stories.headline: Iranian student protests over university-entrance (Konkur) GPA rule spread to about 20 provinces
stories.summary: Thousands of Iranian high-school students protested across roughly 20 provinces on June 6 against a rule making high-school GPA a mandatory share of the Konkur entrance-exam score, which they say undermines educational equality. Demonstrations reached Tehran, Mashhad, Shiraz, Isfahan and Yazd; students were injured in Yazd and arrested in Qom and Mashhad.

perspectives.keyFraming: Quantifies the geographic spread and repression US outlets ignore — some 20 provinces, with arrests and violence over an exam rule.
perspectives.summary: The Times of Israel, citing Iran International, reports students protested in some 20 provinces over exam changes begun in late May; protests reached Tehran, Isfahan, West Azarbaijan, Khuzestan, Fars, Qom, Lorestan and Yazd, with injuries in Yazd and an arrest in Qom; video showed students chanting 'Justice, education, our undeniable right.'

sources.title: Iranian students hold protests in some 20 provinces over exam changes; some met with arrests, violence

perspectives.keyFraming: A non-political grievance over exam fairness snowballs into open youth defiance of the state — a domestic-discontent angle US wires skip while focused on Iran's external conflicts.
perspectives.summary: Iran International reports protests widened over final exams and university-entrance rules, with students staging a sit-in outside Tehran's Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution and chanting against 'empty promises,' vowing not to disperse until their demands are met.

sources.title: Iran student protests widen over final exams and university entrance rules

stories.headline: Three Kuki villagers killed in pre-dawn Manipur attack trigger 35-km coffin rally and district shutdown
stories.summary: Armed assailants attacked Loibol Khullen village in Kangpokpi district of India's Manipur state around 4am on June 5, killing three Kuki-Zo residents and burning seven houses. On June 6 thousands of mourners staged a 35-km coffin rally amid a total district shutdown that blocked the NH-2 highway, demanding justice and central-government action. The killings reignite a Meitei-Kuki ethnic conflict that has run since May 2023.

perspectives.keyFraming: Indian northeastern outlets center the renewed Kuki-Zo grievance and demand New Delhi act — an angle US wires skip entirely.
perspectives.summary: Regional coverage stresses that the victims included a young family, fueling outrage across Kuki-Zo areas, and that the coffin rally carried the message that bloodshed must stop. Reporting situates the attack within the unresolved 2023 ethnic war and ongoing displacement, with Kuki bodies seeking an independent probe and the chief minister condemning the killings.

sources.title: 'Bloodshed Must Stop': Thousands March in Manipur's Kangpokpi as Loibol Victims Laid to Rest
Three Killed, Seven Houses Burnt in Suspected Militant Attack in Manipur's Kangpokpi

stories.headline: ISIS-aligned ADF kill 40+ in a week of Beni massacres in the same zone battling an Ebola outbreak
stories.summary: ADF fighters, an ISIS-affiliated group, killed more than 40 civilians in under a week across Beni territory in North Kivu, DR Congo. A June 2 night raid on Mbau left at least 24 dead, including a pastor's family and schoolchildren, after a May 30-31 wave killed roughly 15 more. The killings hit the same area responders are fighting a Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak, worsening displacement while world attention stays fixed on the M23/Rwanda front to the south.

perspectives.keyFraming: After 40+ deaths in a week, Kinshasa says it is reinforcing security and aid in Ituri and North Kivu — a response US wires leave unreported.
perspectives.summary: As of June 7 the Congolese government said it was bolstering security operations and humanitarian aid in Ituri and North Kivu in the face of repeated ADF raids, after the group's freedom to strike villages within roughly 20 km of Beni City exposed the limits of the joint FARDC-UPDF (Operation Shujaa) campaign and MONUSCO presence.

sources.title: Ituri et Nord-Kivu: Kinshasa renforce sécurité et aide humanitaire face aux ADF

perspectives.keyFraming: Pan-African and rights outlets center the ADF's targeting of Beni villages and a security vacuum US desks omit by framing eastern DRC only through M23-vs-Rwanda.
perspectives.summary: Coverage documents back-to-back overnight raids (Ngadi May 30, Mbau June 2) attributed to the ADF/ISCAP, with 40+ dead in days, pastors and children among the victims, villages sacked and fresh displacement — all in territory simultaneously under an Ebola public-health emergency that the fighting and population movement directly worsen.

sources.title: Massacre in Beni: ADF kills at least 17 civilians in less than 24 hours

stories.headline: Bolivia's Congress passes law letting President Paz deploy troops to clear protest roadblocks
stories.summary: On June 7 Bolivia's Chamber of Deputies passed legislation letting President Rodrigo Paz deploy the military to dismantle road blockades erected by anti-government protesters; the Senate had already approved it. The measure follows weeks of protests over inflation and Paz's fuel-subsidy cuts, with around 100 blockades causing food, fuel and medicine shortages, at least nine reported deaths and over $1.6 billion in estimated losses. Protesters now demand Paz's resignation.

perspectives.keyFraming: A right-wing government barely six months old is being authorized to put soldiers on the streets against a worker-led uprising it cannot otherwise break.
perspectives.summary: Regional outlets foreground the social-class character of the revolt — miners, farmers and transport unions — the human toll of at least nine dead, the logistical siege of La Paz and El Alto, and the constitutional gravity of militarizing a domestic protest response, framing the troops law as a turning point that risks state violence.

sources.title: Bolivia's legislature passes law allowing use of troops against protesters
Bolivian Congress gives Paz power to use troops against crippling protests

perspectives.keyFraming: Economic collapse and fuel-subsidy cuts, not ideology, are driving Bolivians into the streets against a government barely six months old.
perspectives.summary: European coverage emphasizes the economic roots — runaway inflation and subsidy removal — and a deepening cabinet crisis, situating the troops law as a sign the new administration is losing control rather than asserting it, even as Washington's defense secretary has characterized the unrest as a coup attempt.

sources.title: Bolivian Congress gives Paz power to use troops against crippling protests

stories.headline: Mexican cartels turn South African farms into industrial meth labs as fourth major bust reaches court
stories.summary: Investigators say Sinaloa- and Jalisco-linked Mexican syndicates are 'franchising' crystal-meth production onto remote South African farms, sending chemists to build industrial labs near African and European markets rather than only trafficking through the continent. Four major Mexican-linked labs have been found since 2024; the latest, a roughly $60m facility in North West province's Swartruggens, has five Mexicans facing court.

perspectives.keyFraming: Centers Africa as the new manufacturing frontier of the global drug economy and the local corruption that enables it — not as a US domestic cartel-war story.
perspectives.summary: Police uncovered a billion-rand lab on a farm in Swartruggens, the fourth Mexican-linked industrial meth site in two years, with hundreds of kilograms of meth seized and arrests including Mexican nationals. Experts frame this as cartels franchising lab expertise to cut transport and border risk, exploiting rural isolation and police corruption, positioning South Africa as a production base feeding Europe.

sources.title: How Mexican cartels turned South Africa's farms into meth production hubs
